result.markdown: --- title: "[Validate.f_passed()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Provides a dictionary of the fraction of test units that passed for each validation step. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/posit-dev/pointblank/blob/main/pointblank/validate.py#L17300-L17383){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python Validate.f_passed(i=None, scalar=False) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} A measure of the fraction of test units that passed is provided by the `f_passed` attribute. This is the fraction of test units that passed the validation step over the total number of test units. Given this is a fractional value, it will always be in the range of `0` to `1`. Test units are the atomic units of the validation process. Different validations can have different numbers of test units. For example, a validation that checks for the presence of a column in a table will have a single test unit. A validation that checks for the presence of a value in a column will have as many test units as there are rows in the table. This method provides a dictionary of the fraction of passing test units for each validation step. If the `scalar=True` argument is provided and `i=` is a scalar, the value is returned as a scalar instead of a dictionary. Furthermore, a value obtained here will be the complement to the analogous value returned by the [`f_failed()`](`pointblank.Validate.f_failed`) method (i.e., `1 - f_failed()`). ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [i]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int | list[int] | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : The validation step number(s) from which the fraction of passing test units is obtained. Can be provided as a list of integers or a single integer. If `None`, all steps are included. [scalar]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [bool]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[False]{.va}]{.doc-parameter-default} : If `True` and `i=` is a scalar, return the value as a scalar instead of a dictionary. ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [dict[int, float] | float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A dictionary of the fraction of passing test units for each validation step or a scalar value. ::: ## Examples {.doc-examples} In the example below, we'll use a simple Polars DataFrame with three columns (`a`, `b`, and `c`). There will be three validation steps, all having some failing test units. After interrogation, the `f_passed()` method is used to determine the fraction of passing test units for each validation step. ::: {#274d8093 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pointblank as pb import polars as pl tbl = pl.DataFrame( { "a": [7, 4, 9, 7, 12, 3, 10], "b": [9, 8, 10, 5, 10, 6, 2], "c": ["a", "b", "c", "a", "b", "d", "c"] } ) validation = ( pb.Validate(data=tbl) .col_vals_gt(columns="a", value=5) .col_vals_gt(columns="b", value=pb.col("a")) .col_vals_in_set(columns="c", set=["a", "b"]) .interrogate() ) validation.f_passed() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` {1: 0.7142857142857143, 2: 0.5714285714285714, 3: 0.5714285714285714} ``` ::: ::: The returned dictionary shows the fraction of passing test units for each validation step. The values are all less than `1` since there were failing test units in each step. If we wanted to check the fraction of passing test units for a single validation step, we can provide the step number. Also, we could have the value returned as a scalar by setting `scalar=True` (ensuring that `i=` is a scalar). ::: {#34c31bae .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} validation.f_passed(i=1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` {1: 0.7142857142857143} ``` ::: ::: The returned value is the proportion of passing test units for the first validation step (5 passing test units out of 7 total test units).
